findings.description: The Audio Troubleshooting content from PDF page 1 is rendered both as an embedded slide screenshot image and as separate HTML text, creating duplicated content that disrupts reading flow.
findings.evidence: PDF shows the audio troubleshooting panel once as part of the slide layout. HTML shows the same content twice: first as an embedded image of the original slide (including the audio troubleshooting text and screenshots), then repeated below as extracted HTML text ('If you are not hearing any sound, try the following…', etc.).

findings.description: PDF page 1 slide is embedded as a screenshot image in the HTML rather than being purely converted to semantic text/elements. The image contains both the audio troubleshooting panel and the cork-board welcome panel as a single composite screenshot.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 is a presentation slide with two side-by-side panels. The HTML includes what appears to be the original slide image alongside separately extracted text, rather than a clean semantic conversion.

findings.description: PDF page 3 has approximately 5 distinct wildlife/marine photos stacked vertically along the right edge (egret, blue fish, oysters/shellfish, a marine creature such as horseshoe crab or turtle, and an underwater fish scene). The HTML only contains 3 tags for these, meaning approximately 2 substantive images are missing.
findings.evidence: PDF page 3 right sidebar shows ~5 stacked photos. HTML has only 3 img tags: alt='White egret bird in wetland habitat', alt='Collage of marine life including fish, oysters, and scallops', alt='Underwater view of a fish in marine habitat'.

findings.description: Alt text for the second wildlife image describes it as a 'Collage of marine life including fish, oysters, and scallops,' but in the PDF these appear to be separate stacked photos, not a collage. The term 'scallops' may also be inaccurate as the PDF appears to show oysters or other shellfish.
findings.evidence: HTML: alt='Collage of marine life including fish, oysters, and scallops'. PDF shows separate stacked individual photos, not a combined collage image.
